data.title: Chart Of The Day 2/13/26: 'Flash Crashes' Still Plaguing This Market
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It happened again yesterday. A silver “Flash Crash” that sent the metal down 11% in minutes. Gold suffered a similar (though smaller) slump, while equities sold off all day long. That volatility is keeping traders on edge... and rightfully so.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the intraday trading action in spot Bitcoin and silver, gold, and S&P 500 E-mini futures yesterday morning and early afternoon. You can see that this was a cross-asset-class move, even as the percentage declines were smaller outside of silver.

Bitcoin, Silver, S&P 500 E-minis, Gold (Thursday Intraday Chart)

(Source: TradingView)

What happened? Was it a broader “risk-out” move? Part of a big rotation trade into bonds? A reaction to recent AI-driven washouts in SaaS stocks and financial services providers, plus yesterday's wipeout in trucking names?

I didn’t see any great explanations myself. But I do know one thing: seemingly unprovoked sudden, violent moves in any asset class can shake confidence. When you see them in multiple markets, that’s even more concerning.

I’ve been saying for a while that the first quarter of 2026 - and maybe the entire first half - will be choppier than we've gotten used to. So have MoneyShow experts like Jeff Hirsch. With market action now validating that forecast, be sure to account for it in your trading plan!

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Past performance does not guarantee future results. Invested capital is at risk of loss. Please read the above performance in conjunction with the disclosures on the last page of this report. All performance and data shown are in US dollar terms, unless otherwise noted.

What's on Our Minds

For most of the past 15 years, US equities didn’t just outperform, they made their outperformance feel inevitable. That’s what the idea of “US exceptionalism” became in practice: exceptional earnings growth, exceptional valuations (that didn’t matter), and eventually, exceptional concentration of market returns. Which has increasingly narrowed to a heavy bet on AI.

A whole generation of investors has been weaned on US stock market exceptionalism. On a rolling three-year basis, the most recent stretch of US outperformance has lasted approximately 193 months, or more than 15 years—more than two-and-a-half times longer than any prior episode. Such investors may assume that the underperformance of US equities in 2025 was an aberration soon to be reversed, but market structure has changed from 15 years ago. While valuations of US and international markets were once similar, the US now trades at nearly twice the multiples of foreign stocks. The valuation gap has widened, in part, due to the rising market values of the largest “AI plays” in the US that represent a larger proportion of the overall market. By year-end 2025, the top 10 US stocks by market capitalization represented roughly 40% of the MSCI USA Index, up from about 18% in 2009. Outside the US, however, concentration rose only modestly, with the top 10 members of the MSCI ACWI ex US moving from roughly 10% to 13% of the index’s value.

That widening gap in valuations and market diversity matters because it changes what US investors are currently pricing in and ultimately betting on. At today’s starting point, continued US market leadership increasingly depends on a single premise: that AI will produce an extended expansion of growth and profitability, most of it captured by a select few US companies.

But AI isn’t a monolithic trade; it’s a capital-expenditure regime with two distinct camps of participants, and two very different risk profiles. On the demand side are the AI capex investors: hyperscalers and platform companies committing large portions of cash flow to build compute capacity at scale. On the supply side are the physical enablers of AI: the global ecosystem that makes its buildout possible, spanning chip foundries, memory-chip makers, equipment manufacturers, as well as providers of power management, cooling, and the specialized industrial infrastructure required to deploy and operate data centers.

Despite the size of NVIDIA, the US equity market is disproportionately weighted toward the first group: the hyperscalers and the AI platform cohort. Alphabet, Amazon.com, Meta Platforms, and Microsoft are the largest buyers of AI computing power, committing an increasing share of their cash flow to data centers and infrastructure. Their cumulative spend is widely expected to reach hundreds of billions of dollars over the next several years. This is the heart of the US AI narrative: if you believe large capex inevitably translates into even larger monetization, the US currently dominates those profit pools.

International markets, by contrast, are more heavily tilted toward the second group: the ecosystem of manufacturers and infrastructure providers. While the US-listed NVIDIA may dominate accelerator economics, it’s a fabless chipmaker, meaning it can’t deliver a single graphics processing unit (GPU) unless the likes of Taiwan’s TSMC fabricates it. Amazon, Meta, or Microsoft may want the biggest data centers with unparalleled computing capabilities, but not one gets built without power-management systems or components from the likes of Delta Electronics or Schneider Electric. The US AI leaders’ products and services sit atop a deeply international supply chain—foundries, memory, semiconductor equipment, precision manufacturing, and the power and thermal systems required to run AI models at scale. In other words, non-US companies may include fewer of the headline AI platforms, but are key physical enablers of the buildout.

Market Snapshot

  • The MSCI ACWI ex US index outpaced the MSCI USA index in the quarter and for the full year. Cheaper starting valuations, tariff and policy uncertainty in the US, and a decline in the US dollar supported better returns for international equities.
  • Canada outperformed this quarter, as Financials there benefited from an improving yield curve, and gold and silver mining stocks continued to surge. Emerging Markets were bifurcated: China posted solid gains over the year but declined in the quarter amid concerns over slowing domestic demand, partially offsetting strong returns across much of the rest of EM, led by South Korea.
  • AI-related capital expenditures lifted Information Technology returns, with gains concentrated in semiconductor and semiconductor equipment stocks. Utilities also outperformed, particularly in Europe, supported by rising data-center power needs and increased grid investment.
  • Health Care rebounded on strong performance from pharmaceutical and biotechnology stocks. Sentiment improved following agreements between several companies and the Trump administration to provide discounted drugs to the Medicaid program, reducing policy uncertainty.
  • Communication Services declined, dragged down by media and entertainment and weakness in several large Chinese internet platforms. Index heavyweight Tencent fell sharply during the quarter, despite continued growth in online gaming and advertising revenues.
  • The cheapest quintile of stocks outperformed the most expensive quintile by nearly 12 percentage points. For the year, the performance gap between these cohorts was a staggering 46 percentage points.

Although the AI bubble question will continue to be asked in 2026, there isn’t a simple answer to the question. Businesses investing in AI infrastructure need to build customer bases and generate enough revenue to justify their investments. If that revenue falls short of expectations, that’s bad not just for those companies but also their suppliers, for while AI-related investment demand won’t disappear overnight, it could slow. If that were to happen, the pain will show up first in the most cyclical parts of the industry: semiconductor equipment and certain parts of the manufacturing chain, then foundries and component suppliers, and only later in the more insulated platform economics.

Both US and international investors have a stake in AI’s growth. But if the AI cycle slows, at the stock level the US market faces a double sensitivity: rich starting valuations and a large share of index returns that hinge on a narrow cohort. Therefore, the US market is more dependent on AI continuing to surprise to the upside to justify its premium. Non-US markets would not be immune, particularly the aforementioned group of more cyclical suppliers, but they start from cheaper valuations and possess a more diverse set of growth opportunities wholly unrelated to AI.

In several cases, share prices lagged even as underlying earnings continued to grow, an important reminder that the pace of AI adoption and the trajectory of AI equity returns aren’t necessarily synchronized in the short run.

This is also where policy becomes more than a footnote. When valuations are demanding and market leadership is narrow, policy volatility carries a higher price. Fifteen years ago, the US was more exceptional in terms of being a haven for global capital. Then, it enjoyed an AAA sovereign credit rating and ranked top ten globally in the US-based Heritage Foundation’s Index of Economic Freedom, reflecting a policy environment broadly supportive of capital formation and business activity. By 2025, the US fell to 26th on that list. Elevated uncertainty around tariffs, regulation, fiscal direction, and monetary policy doesn’t need to completely derail the AI story to matter; it simply needs to raise risk premiums and widen the range of outcomes for the most crowded part of the market.

So how do we respond as investors, especially when diversification has felt like a penalty at times?

In our portfolio, we own many companies that are direct beneficiaries of the AI buildout: semiconductor manufacturing leaders and equipment suppliers, and companies exposed to the power and thermal infrastructure AI requires. These include TSMC, the world’s largest semiconductor foundry; Samsung Electronics, the leading memory producer; semiconductor manufacturing equipment suppliers such as ASML, Disco Corp, Lasertec, and Daifuku; and companies addressing AI’s substantial power and thermal management needs, including Delta Electronics and Schneider Electric. And while the US has the hyperscalers making massive investments in data centers, we own Tencent in China and Naver in South Korea, both of which are investing in data center infrastructure to support AI computation serving regional customer bases, albeit with significantly lower levels of capital expenditure commitments. These holdings have participated meaningfully in the upside of AI-related investment, while giving us exposure to the critical layer outside US mega-caps. The above-mentioned companies, representing nearly a fifth of our portfolio would be considered “AI plays” by market pundits— roughly half what the US index bet is, but above what our international benchmark holds.

We’ve also sought more diverse ways to participate in AI and digitization beyond the most cyclical parts of the supply chain, with software and services businesses that are applying AI to improve their products, automate processes, and expand margins. Results here were mixed in 2025. In several cases, share prices lagged even as underlying earnings continued to grow, an important reminder that the pace of AI adoption and the trajectory of AI equity returns aren’t necessarily synchronized in the short run.

The more useful question isn’t whether an AI bubble exists but rather where expectations are most stretched—and which stocks bear the greatest downside if the capex cycle cools. And the more actionable question is, where is the market’s dependence concentrated, and what is already priced in? US exceptionalism has become tightly linked to an AI capex regime, and the US index is unusually exposed to concentrated bets facing a wide range of outcomes. International equities, meanwhile, otter participation in the same structural buildout through a greater variety of business models, often at more attractive valuations, with less single-factor concentration, and with a wider range of non-AI sources of growth.

If AI delivers on its promise, global winners will continue to extend well beyond US mega-caps. And if the capex cycle proves bumpier than the market expects, diversification across the chain and across regions may matter more than it has in years.

Portfolio in Focus

Turnover in the portfolio was higher in 2025, at 24% nearly double the average of the previous five years, as we sought to capitalize on equity-market dislocations caused by sharp swings in risk appetite. Activity in the fourth quarter was less than the prior two quarters, but continued in the same direction: we increased exposure to Emerging Markets, further lifted the portfolio’s growth exposure by reducing Materials and Consumer Staples in favor of Communication Services, and added selectively to European defense following a pullback.

Over the past decade we have maintained an underweight in Emerging Markets—averaging five percentage points less than the benchmark—not due to a top-down view on the region, but as an outcome of our bottom-up assessment of growth, quality, valuation, and risk across the international opportunity set. While many EM companies can offer faster topline growth and higher business quality metrics such as asset returns and low leverage, those attributes often comes with a trade-off in risk—including country risk, corporate governance, and management capital allocation. When those risks are reflected in higher discount rates, fewer EM companies have historically met our risk-reward analysis relative to developed-market peers. Over the past several years, underperformance and compressed valuations relative to developed markets has created opportunities in stocks of a number of businesses where fundamentals remain robust, including CATL, Delta Electronics, Naver, SF Holding, and NetEase, as well as DM-listed but EM-focused e-commerce operators Sea Limited and MercadoLibre. Good relative performance in EMs and our additions pushed our EM holdings to roughly 30%, their largest weight ever in the portfolio.

Naver (NHNCF), one of South Korea’s leading search platforms and the country’s second-largest e-commerce business, illustrates the trade-offs. Naver is investing in AI capabilities that can expand its revenue opportunities across both advertising and e-commerce by following the same playbook that Alphabet and Meta used in Western markets—making digital advertising both easier and cheaper. AI-based targeting, measurement, and automated ad-generation tools will allow Naver to help small and medium businesses which historically have spent less on Naver advertising and e-commerce because they lacked the know-how to use it effectively and found the cost of using digital advertising too high. While these investments will dilute profit margins in the near term, they should improve Naver’s long-term profitability and free-cash-flow generation as products scale up, capex plateaus, and monetization broadens. Against that backdrop, Naver shares trade at a meaningful discount to global internet platforms in our modeling of fair value as well as on traditional metrics such as earnings multiples, creating an attractive entry point for the investment we made this quarter.

Another new holding this quarter is BAE Systems (BAESY). BAE, formerly British Aerospace, is among the largest defense contractors outside the US or China, with capabilities across air, land, sea, space, and electronic systems, and the scale to serve multiple geographies. Its intellectual property, long-standing government relationships, and track record of program execution have supported resilient profitability even through long years of restrained European defense spending. With indications of sustained higher defense budgets in Europe, alongside a push to modernize in the US, BAE’s order book has strengthened while profitability has remained steady. We were able to buy shares amid a broader sell-off in European defense stocks, as investors wavered over a potential Ukraine–Russia settlement that could reduce near-term order momentum.

Good relative performance in EM and our additions pushed our EM holdings to roughly 30%, their largest weight ever in the portfolio.

To fund the investments in these higher-growth companies, we reduced exposure elsewhere. We sold long-standing holdings in Linde (LIN) and BHP (BHP), reducing our Materials weight to less than 5% of the portfolio. Linde’s valuation looked stretched against its high-single-digit long-term growth outlook. Meanwhile, BHP’s growth outlook appeared to deteriorate, with rising Australia–China frictions increasing risks around iron ore sales to Chinese steel manufacturers, who have built ample stockpiles of ore, yet face moribund demand growth. We also sold German enterprise resource planning software provider SAP, whose demanding valuation discounts a degree of growth acceleration unlikely to materialize.

Overall, these changes increased the portfolio’s growth profile without a meaningful sacrifice in quality, and at more reasonable valuations than a year ago. We end 2025 with a portfolio that betters the index in cash flow generation, a key driver of long-term outcomes, with a portfolio-weighted median five-year cash flow growth rate of 12.8% versus 9.5% for the index. The consensus 2026 earnings growth outlook for the portfolio of 17.6% is also materially higher than 13.0% for the index. As has long been the case, our portfolio of faster-growing and higher-quality companies comes at a premium valuation, but after the last four years of crushing style headwinds, that premium sits near multi-year lows, (as shown in the chart above), offering an attractive outlook for the portfolio.

Performance and Attribution

The International Equity composite returned 4.4% gross of fees in the fourth quarter, not far behind the 5.1% gain in the MSCI ACWI ex US Index. For the year, however, the composite rose 28.4% gross of fees, lagging the index return of 33.1%. The portfolio’s emphasis on higher-quality companies, and consequently its light holdings of the cheapest cohort of stocks, hurt performance across the board.

Portfolio Attribution by Sector

Sector allocation was modestly helpful in the fourth quarter, particularly in IT and Consumer Discretionary, but stock selection detracted across most sectors.

Top contributors to relative performance:

  • Our Financials stocks bettered the index in the fourth quarter, led by insurers Ping An Insurance and Manulife, both of which have seen a reacceleration of new business in China and Hong Kong, respectively.
  • For the full year, we had good stock selection and a modest overweight in the top-performing IT sector, led by technology hardware and equipment companies such as Delta Electronics and Samsung Electronics, as well as semiconductor maker TSMC.
  • Strong returns from Industrials stocks CATL, the Chinese battery specialist who reported battery shipments and earnings growth rates at close to 40%, and Ryanair, the Irish low-cost airline, which posted record-high quarterly profits in the third quarter, helped full-year performance.

Top detractors from relative performance:

  • In the fourth quarter, our IT stocks lagged, as our software holdings trailed the very strong returns of the sector, dominated by soaring shares of Korean memory producers and other semiconductor-related companies. Samsung Electronics, which managed to increase shipments of high-bandwidth memory to NVIDIA in the quarter, rose, partially offsetting our lagging stocks.
  • For the full year, our Health Care stocks were battered by a more hostile political and regulatory environment developing in the US, the biggest profit pool for the global industry, and by further valuation compression for many of the faster-growing equipment and services companies such as Sysmex. Our hefty allocation to the sector also hurt.
  • In Industrials, Swedish multinationals Atlas Copco and Alfa Laval, along with France’s Schneider Electric detracted from relative performance for the full year, with each still among the more richly priced capital goods stocks, and with growth that has wobbled amid uncertainty for capital investment outside of AI.

Portfolio Attribution by Region

For the fourth quarter, poor stocks in Japan were offset by good stocks in EMs and Europe. Poor Japanese stock performance dominated full-year results, while EMs outperformed.

Top contributors to relative performance:

By selection effect, EMs were our strongest region in the fourth quarter, with strong stocks in China including Ping An Insurance, as well as Samsung Electronics in South Korea.

Europe ex EMU helped fourth quarter performance, as Swiss pharmaceutical Roche gained on strong performance of its current drugs and positive readouts on its future pipeline, and our Swedish stocks recovered from prior weakness as operational reports remained resilient.

Our large IT holdings in Taiwan and South Korea described above, as well as Mexico’s GF Banorte and China’s Ping An Insurance, contributed to our full-year returns in EMs. Hong Kong-listed AIA Group and Singapore’s DBS Group helped in Pacific ex Japan.

Top detractors from relative performance:

Japan was among the biggest detractors in the fourth quarter and the biggest detractor for the full year due to the drag from more richly priced, higher-quality companies including Keyence, Sysmex, and Unicharm.

Europe and Canada also weighed on full-year returns, largely due to the dramatic outperformance of cheap, lower-quality banks and miners we didn’t own.

data.title: Crocs Update Post Q4 Earnings - Still A Cheap Buy
data.description:
Introduction

In my last article on Crocs (CROX), I recommended buying the stock while it was still cheap. After today's strong earnings report, CROX is up 19% to $98.46 per share. The good news, however, is that the stock remains cheap. (If you're interested in a deep dive into Crocs' business, check out my prior article here).

In this article I will cover what we learned from the latest earnings announcement and update my forecast and price target for the stock. I reiterate my strong buy rating and upgrade the price target to $173 per share.

Q4 Earnings Main Takeaways

First, the company beat expectations on both the top and bottom lines (source: Q4 Earnings Call). Adjusted EPS was $2.29 vs. $1.91 expected, and revenues were $958 million vs. $916.1 million expected. Guidance was also stronger than expected, at $12.88-$13.35 EPS for the year ahead vs. Wall Street's estimate of $12.10.

In my prior article I said the following:

I believe Crocs' shareholders would be well-served by the company continuing to pay down debts. Interest expenses are currently about $100 million per year, which at the moment is eating up about 13% of free cash flow... With share prices as depressed as they are, I think it also makes sense for the company to continue with the authorized share repurchases. Crocs jumped the gun in my view, repurchasing shares near all-time highs in the last two years, but now (if my analysis is correct) the shares are truly undervalued, which makes share repurchases a smart way for the company to use its cash.

I was pleased to see both significant debt repayments and share repurchases in Q4. In this quarter alone, Crocs repaid $90 million of debt and repurchased 2.2 million shares for $180 million. Interest expense for the year decreased by 19.2%, or a little over $20 million. The company still has significant runway in both of these areas with remaining share repurchase authorization of $746.8 million and debt obligations of $1.26 billion.

Management also expects HEYDUDE revenue to stabilize this year. HEYDUDE revenues were down 17% YoY, but they were up slightly quarter-over-quarter, indicating a bottoming out for the brand. The company believes it's reached a point where HEYDUDE inventory troubles are in the rearview mirror. CEO Andrew Rees outlined the HEYDUDE strategy:

HEYDUDE, we worked really hard and spent quite a lot of money in the back half of last year to rightsize inventories. And we think we're there, right? We look at our sellout relative to our inventories on hand, and they are at parity at this point. So we're excited to be at that point and be able to strategically rebuild the business from a wholesale perspective for HEYDUDE.

International direct-to-consumer sales grew 23%, with China specifically growing 30%. Rees says they're confident in a 10% growth rate in the international business going forward.

Additionally, $50 million in cost savings were implemented in 2025 with another $100 million in cost savings currently being implemented in 2026, which should improve the company's margins.

Gross margins were down 50 basis points for the year, but tariffs caused 130 basis points of decline. So absent the effects of tariffs, margins actually improved by 80 basis points. Gross margins for the core brand were only down 30 basis points, vs. a 280 basis point gross margin deterioration in the HEYDUDE brand.

Valuation Update

I updated my FCF valuation model from my previous article to incorporate the following changes:

  • FCF to revenue margins expanding slightly through 2030 rather than staying flat (to reflect ongoing cost savings).
  • Net debt decreasing due to continued debt repayments
  • Shares outstanding decreasing due to share repurchases.

Incorporating these changes, the updated fair value per share is $173, still a 76% upside to current market value. One thing to note is the very high short interest in this stock at 11.64%, which could be a potential re-pricing tailwind. If my thesis is correct, then sooner or later shorts will have to cover their positions.

I also want to highlight CROX strong profitability relative to the Consumer Discretionary sector:

Crocs beats the sector on nearly every metric of profitability. One of my new favorite metrics, "Net Income Per Employee", shows that Crocs generates $23k net income per employee compared to $12.3k for the sector average. Note that that figure is based on 2025's Net Income which included asset write-downs and impairments. Using the guidance for 2026 and a conservative estimate of 8000 current employees, Crocs would be expected to generate closer to $85k net income per employee, about seven times the average for the Consumer Discretionary sector.

Conclusion

I previously dubbed Crocs a "Tale of Two Brands". What we see at the moment is one brand moving forward at a steady, reliable pace and the other brand getting back on track after a period of instability. Even if Crocs does no better than sustain its present free cash flows in perpetuity, the stock is still undervalued. It remains priced for significant deterioration, when there is still no strong evidence of that happening. Even with the 19% jump in the share price today, forward P/E is only 7.5x relative to the midpoint EPS guidance.

All the evidence I see points in the opposite direction. The core Crocs brand has strong growth internationally, and sales have been stabilizing in the North American core brand and HEYDUDE brand. Generally companies that are in decline don't have such strong profitability metrics. If Crocs business was really deteriorating, I would expect to see some evidence of it in margin compression beyond just expected fallout due to tariffs.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Trivago: Proving Doubters Wrong
data.description:
Trivago (TRVG) is a stock I have been following for a while in the online travel space. TRVG is a hotel metasearch engine, which connects users to hotel booking sites, and not an OTA (online travel agents) like Expedia (EXPE).

I first covered the stock in February 2024, when I rated the stock a buy when it was trading around $2.45 per share. Though the stock failed to reach my 1-year target price of $5.9 per share by February 2025, it would then test a 1-year high of $5.4 per share the following month in March 2025, implying that my undervaluation thesis was right. After experiencing some volatility, TRVG today trades around $2.85 per share, up 16.3% since my coverage.

I maintain my buy rating. My modeled 1-year target price of $4.2 presents a projected 47% upside from today’s price of $2.85. In times when many doubt travel metasearch’s survival capability in the midst of AI-powered search engines, TRVG’s recent financial success has proven doubters wrong so far.

Financial Reviews

Based on the Q4 2025 release, fundamentals appear improving. In Q4, TRVG delivered a revenue of €120 million, a 27% YoY growth, driven by the 17% YoY growth in referral revenue to €109.4 million.

From a FY standpoint, TRVG has also overall seen a strong performance in FY 2025. FY revenue was €549 million, a 19% YoY growth. With TRVG also expecting another double-digit growth in FY 2026, the business appears to have seen momentum, suggesting that the turnaround strategy a few years ago has enabled the company to overcome the post-COVID stagnation that has depressed its valuation.

In Q4, TRVG also saw better profitability YoY across the board. In Q4, net income was €14.5 million, while adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) was €11.3 million. While the AI-driven product improvement helped stronger conversion rates, as per the management’s comment in earnings call, one drawback from the quarter was the lower ROAS (return on ad-spend), which was due to aggressive marketing:

Our core hotel search product continues to advance quickly. In 2025, we have improved our conversion reaching 37% increase versus 2023, materially enhancing our unit economics. These gains are powered by AI and hundreds of experiments each quarter.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Liquidity position appeared solid in Q4, with TRVG ending the FY with €130.9 million in cash and cash equivalents with zero debts, while also consistently delivering positive operating cash flow (OCF) over the past few years. In Q4, OCF was €19.3 million.

Catalyst

I found catalysts to be moderate to strong for TRVG, primarily driven by the branded marketing and continued AI-driven product innovation. In Q4, the company also provided some insights into the performance of and the progress of its hotel search improvements and marketing campaigns.

Given the significantly strong results in terms of conversion, I have a decent amount of confidence that TRVG’s branded marketing and product-led growth are currently helping the company to see an improved competitive positioning against its competitors, such as Kayak or Tripadvisor.

Driven by the strong AI adoption across the consumers’ end, I believe that TRVG is in a good position to continue benefiting from AI technology, especially since its investments in 2024 is already proven to generate financial impact today through better search experience.

In the end, in my opinion, given the clear catalysts into FY 2026, TRVG could potentially see a re-rating sometime in the FY, especially with the share price continuing to trade at a relatively low EV/Revenue today, despite the double-digit growth and expanding aEBITDA.

Risk

Meanwhile, I believe risk remains moderate. My biggest concern here would be the competition from Google’s recently-launched AI-driven travel search tools, instead of from competitors like Tripadvisor or Kayak.

The main reason I believe TRVG’s investments into branded marketing is a right move is because it should improve top-of-mind effect that reduces the dependency on search engine-based conversion, as per the management’s comment:

So we have reduced dependencies on performance marketing channels or on search channels over the course of the year, which was an important step as well, and we will continue to be disciplined on those channels. And I think on the brand channel, we have become more diversified, which was also an objective to get confidence that we have avenues to grow in brands and with a high elasticity when we incrementally invest.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

However, with Google investing aggressively into its AI chatbot and agents, integrating them into the traditional search experience, and then deciding to enhance some of the use cases like hotel or flight booking, then there is a risk where Google simply replaces the metasearch platforms in the worst case scenario in the future, in my opinion.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for TRVG is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on consensus estimate. Based on the 5-to-1 ratio of shares outstanding to ADR shares, weighted shares outstanding is estimated to be between 70 to 75 million:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions - I project TRVG to maintain double-digit revenue growth, while aEBITDA exceeding €25 million, driven by stronger AI-driven conversion. TRVG becomes leading top-of-mind travel metasearch across more geographies by year’s end, leading to a soft re-rating to 0.5x P/S, up from today’s 0.3x.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions - I expect Google travel search tool to gain massive adoptions, effectively disrupting all metasearch traffic, including that of TRVG, stalling revenue growth to just under 3%. The market continues to be skeptical of the metasearch industry, followed by a rating downgrade to 0.15x P/S.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $4.2 per share, suggesting a 47% upside from the current price level. I rate the stock a buy.

Conclusion

Leveraging AI to drive search conversion, TRVG is undergoing a pivotal transformation. In FY 2025, the shift to profitability as well as double-digit revenue growth may continue to prove doubters wrong about metasearch capability to remain relevant in midst of Google’s expansion into AI-based search experience, which has been a key risk for TRVG, even today. All that said, TRVG seems undervalued today. With the projected double-digit growth and aEBITDA expansion in FY 2026, I lean on the bullish side of things. Even as I bake in the potential financial impact from the risk factor, my target price projection still yields an attractive risk-reward for investors.

data.title: Getty Realty: A High-Yield REIT Still Flying Under The Radar
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Getty Realty (GTY), I highlighted their undervaluation, strong dividend yield and solid financial position despite macro pressure, with an upgraded guidance and very strong ~99.8% occupancy rate that reinforced their Strong Buy rating.

With a strong report released recently that beat the market’s expectations, a good valuation despite their proven resilience, and nearly perfect occupancy rate, I reiterate GTY’s Strong Buy rating, as this small triple net lease REIT continues to be too cheap to ignore, in my opinion.

Internal Developments

GTY reported a strong Q4 and 2025 overall, with the latest report beating the market’s FFO and revenue estimates by quite a bit, which further reinforces their solid performance in what’s otherwise a weak environment.

The company’s AFFO was up by about 8.1% to $141.44 million in 2025, while the AFFO per share increased by 3.8%, with their initial AFFO guidance for 2026 implying a 2.46% increase based on the midpoint of their $2.48 to $2.50 per share.

This comes as the company maintained a very solid 99.7% occupancy, with a 9.9-year weighted average lease term and 99.9% rent collection. GTY also announced that they invested another ~$135 million at a 7.9% initial cash yield in Q4 (including 15 convenience stores, 6 auto service centers and 2 express tunnel car washes), for a total of about $268.8 million at a 7.9% initial cash yield in 2025.

This also marks a 5.1% increase in AFFO per share from 2019 to 2025 (and 4.9% for the dividend), which further shows how resilient and surprisingly stable the company can be even during what was perhaps one of the worst environments for them.

Financially, based on GTY’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position for a REIT, with a manageable amount of debt that’s well-spread throughout the long-term, with no year coming with a significant maturity and no maturity until 2028.

If anything, their June 2028 notes have a 5.47% interest rate, which could potentially result in interest savings if they manage to refinance at better rates by that time, which I wouldn't rule out given their improvements and potential for better macro conditions.

Recently, GTY declared a dividend of $0.485 (in line with the previous one), which represents about $110 million per year, meaning that the dividend remains well-covered, with an AFFO payout ratio of about 78% based on the latest data.

Macro Developments

Despite GTY's solid expansion and results, we have to remember that this is far from a great environment, which makes their performance as a relatively small REIT even more impressive. I want to mention again that this weakness seen in REITs is not something unusual, as these companies ultimately rely significantly on the cost of external capital, which is significantly higher now than before the recent rate hikes.

I believe this is important to understand since we’re talking about an asset class that ultimately depends on the economic cycle, and just because they underperformed in times when they’re supposed to do it, that doesn’t make them bad.

In fact, these were what I’d call the best times to buy them, as I got GTY for example at a yield of 7.4%, which is among the highest we’ve ever seen from them, while other REITs that I covered were bought at even better yields, which I see as very rare opportunities that will be extremely hard to find during a good environment.

For GTY in particular, we can see the recent 5.76% fixed-yield $250 million in new unsecured notes they announced during Q4 that they used to repay borrowings on their credit facility.

This is well above the company's weighted average interest rate of 4.5% now, and it clearly highlights the effects of this high-rate environment, while a higher-for-longer scenario can be even worse for them, as convenience, car wash, and especially drive-thru/QSR tenants reported weakness in recent years, potentially hurting the demand for GTY's properties as these companies tighten the belt or even close underperforming properties.

Although it's slightly better than issuing stock (which comes with a ~5.88% "cost" now after the stock increased), it's still not an amazing difference from their 7.9% initial cash yield compared to the 4.09% (their 2029 notes) or even 3.43% (their 2030 notes) yields they were able to borrow at during a better environment.

Meanwhile, the consumer environment continues to be weak, with several studies highlighting the higher level of stress due to the lower discretionary budgets, job insecurity, higher costs of living, and more, while US consumer confidence recently fell to the lowest level since 2014, which can threaten their tenants' stability and ultimately GTY's gap between their costs of capital and cash yields.

Although improving now thanks to recent economic data and improving market sentiment, treasury yields remain elevated, and it will take much more than some rate cuts from the Fed to reduce the long-term yields.

Other than the “natural” weakness, the market has to take into account many other factors that can affect the country’s growth prospects, including Japan’s economic plans that can affect US treasuries and the dollar combined with the fact that recent news shows its national debt hitting a record in 2025, the falling dollar, renewed geopolitical and tariff fears, crisis worries due to the rising national debt - just to name a few of the recent news that took the spotlight.

Again, even though I believe Kevin Warsh has potential to help as Fed Chair given his past stances (despite some of them changing in recent years, being closer to the President’s now), there are still things that go beyond the Fed’s capabilities even if it was doing its job perfectly, and there is definitely real risk of seeing a higher-for-longer rate environment - at the very least - which can be bad for GTY, its tenants, clients, partners, and the economy as a whole.

Still, I believe we have to look at this from a long-term perspective, and this is where GTY remains a solid, high-yield option to keep in mind, as their properties can benefit significantly from a recovery in the environment, with plenty of room to see improvements while they don’t have near-term maturities to worry about, which can buy them time to wait for better yields before having to refinance.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, GTY’s properties and tenants will always depend significantly on the environment since we're talking about convenience, car washes, drive thrus and auto service centers, which exposes them to consumer weakness that’s hard to overcome given the current costs of capital, especially since there’s definitely room for it to get worse before it gets any better.

Meanwhile, even though they can still wait before having to refinance, a higher-for-longer rate environment can lead to the company eventually being forced to borrow at higher rates, which increases their interest expenses and directly hurts their AFFO and potentially the valuation, while increasing the risk of tenant issues.

Valuation

For GTY’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting with their 2025 AFFO of $141.44 million and $144.53 million in 2026 (midpoint of their guidance), followed by a CAGR of 5.5% until 2030, as I believe they can still expand significantly especially as the environment improves and supports their expansion at better costs, with a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

I’ll go for a 10% discount rate to assume a better margin of safety since they still depend on the economy at the end of the day, with 58.04 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.22 billion, for an intrinsic value of $38.33 per share, which is still above the current $31.07 per share at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

I reiterate GTY’s Strong Buy rating, as the company continues to prove their resilience, strength and potential to grow even during a weak environment, with a solid and sustainable dividend on top.

There are certainly risks, as the company’s properties and tenants remain exposed significantly to the economy, but the overall risk-reward remains in the investment’s favor, in my opinion.

data.title: FLCA: Warrants Broader Interest, But Wait For A Pullback
data.description:
ETF Profile

The Franklin FTSE Canada ETF (FLCA), which has been in existence since November 2017, enables investors to participate in the movements of over 80 large and mid-sized Canadian stocks (although the former category accounts for 90% of the portfolio, with an average market cap of over $60B reiterating this large-cap tilt even further) through the ETF medium.

FLCA, primarily does so, by tracking an index called the FTSE Canada RIC Capped Index [FCRCI] which is a free-float adjusted market-cap weighted index, but also resorts to some caps (each quarter, individual stock caps are maintained at 20%, while all stocks with weights of over 4.5% are not permitted to jointly account for over 48% of the index) to prevent concentration effects.

Even though FLCA is supposed to be a passively managed ETF (it resorts to a combination of both full replication and sampling, at different periods), this ETF’s ability to successfully track FCRCI is hardly pristine, with tracking errors that are slightly more than the ETF median, particularly over the past year. Some of this could also be a function of FLCA’s goal to only hold “at least 80%” of the assets of FCRCI rather than a higher number.

In terms of sector exposure, even though this portfolio offers coverage to 10 different sectors, two key subplots stand out: firstly, an overwhelming chunk of the portfolio consists of financial stocks (38% of the portfolio, which is more than 2x larger than the next biggest sector- materials), and secondly, FLCA offers no exposure to the defensive-healthcare sector.

Is FLCA Better Than Its Larger Peers?

If investors want pure-play unleveraged exposure to Canadian stocks in the form of an ETF, they have the option of pursuing 2 different ETFs besides FLCA which also cover a similar number of stocks (75-85), most of which are financial stocks, and also have average market-caps within the large-cap spectrum.

The first- the iShares MSCI Canada ETF (EWC) is something of a stalwart in this space, having been around for close to three decades now (it was set-up in March 1996)! However, interestingly enough, despite the notable longevity, it isn’t topping the AUM charts with +$4B in AUM; that honor goes to the much younger JPMorgan BetaBuilders Canada ETF (BBCA), which, in less than 8 years (it even got listed 9 months after FLCA), has already managed AUM that is more than double of what EWC has managed, and a whopping 16x more than our ETF in focus- FLCA.

However, popularity shouldn’t be measured in just pure AUM terms, with daily dollar volumes also coming into play. From this perspective, EWC is the king, while FLCA fares quite poorly, witnessing just $2M in volume (on average). The quantum of dollar volumes matters because it results in tighter spreads, and reduces the risk of slippages. EWC’s spreads are rather tight at 0.02%, but FLCA could certainly do a lot better with spreads of 0.15%.

All three portfolios are very stable, witnessing low annual churn of single digits, but BBCA just about nudges it with a churn rate that is only half as much as the other two. FLCA edges it on the income front, with a slightly better yield than BBCA, although investors who prefer more frequent distributions may gravitate to the latter. EWC compares poorly on this front.

If there’s one area that FLCA shines, it is on the affordability front, as it is priced at just 9bps, which is not even half as much as it costs to own BBCA (also not even one-fifth of EWC).

Also, even though past returns are not indicative of what will happen in the future, investors may also be enthused to note that the small fry has outperformed both its larger peers, since the youngest, BBCA, got listed.

Comfortingly, these superior returns have also come despite FLCA incurring lower average rolling volatility (16.48%) than the other two products.

To conclude this section, so long as investors are prepared to overlook the relatively wide spreads, we feel FLCA should be seen as the preferred ETF route to play Canada, as it is cheaper, offers better yields, and has a better track record of risk-adjusted returns.

Is FLCA Worth Pursuing Now?

While FLCA gets our vote over its more popular Canadian peers, should investors pursue this product now? Well, that depends on what sort of an investor you are.

If you’re drawn to themes of momentum, and income, an investment in FLCA now may entice you. We say this because, the ETF is currently experiencing a series of higher highs [HHs] and higher lows [HLs] and trading above all its key moving averages.

From an income perspective, we’ve already highlighted how FLCA offers the best yield over the other two alternatives; looking ahead to 2026, we feel that FLCA’s financial- and materials heavy portfolio will likely benefit from stronger capital returns this year.

Considering the tariff-related risks that the Canadian economy has to deal with, it is questionable if loan growth appetite will be particularly meaningful for these Canadian banks. However, do also note that they are quite well stocked on the capital front; compared to a common equity tier 1 regulatory requirement of at least 11.5%, the big six banks of Canada (all of which are part of FLCA and with some pretty big weights) are all maintaining a higher threshold, which can potentially be given out by way of dividends or higher share buybacks (although given the substantial price rise, and relatively expensive valuations, it doesn’t make sense to double down on buybacks)

FLCA’s material stocks as well are benefitting from strong commodity and metal prices that should help boost cash flows that could be returned.

While all this is very well, value conscious investors may not find FLCA too appealing now, as it is priced at a 17x earnings and 2.5x book. Those multiples represent a premium of 15% and 32%, respectively, vs other developed markets.

Considering these steep valuations, we could potentially see fund flows move out of Canada into other developed markets, potentially bringing down FLCA’s relative strength ratio (vs. other developed market stocks) to its long-term mean. To elaborate, at this juncture, the current RS is 11% higher than its long-term mean.

Finally, if we look at FLCA’s own monthly price imprints, we can see that across most of its lifetime, it trended higher within the boundaries of an ascending channel. FLCA did well to break out from this channel in November, but is now trading well above the upper channel, and is yet to see any pullback (we know equities don’t move in a linear manner and are always prone to retracements). The more sensible stance would be to wait for FLCA to revisit the old upper boundary of the channel, and defend those levels, before diving in, as currently even the RSI indicator (at the lower end of the chart) is flagging overbought conditions.

data.title: IXC: Hold As Energy Breadth Peaks
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

Seeking exposure to the American energy sector may make sense if you think we are at a good point in the energy business cycle. Doing so in an indexed way is an interesting option for newer investors or those who do not want to worry about the daily management of their investment portfolio. To do this, it is appropriate to study a market alternative such as that proposed by Blackrock with its listed vehicle iShares Global Energy ETF (IXC).

This index fund is managed passively, since it tries to replicate before commissions and expenses the behavior of the S&P Global 1200 Energy Capped Index. In turn, the index is composed of companies with sensitivity to the business cycle, the transport cycle and ultimately the price of oil (Brent). An extra curiosity is that the index is not only exposed to American companies, but it is a global index, with a built-in weight limitation procedure per company, a mechanism that is incorporated so as not to incur any kind of concentration bias.

The main variables that affect the behavior of the ETF and the companies inside it are the price of Brent, the evolution of the dollar and other currencies (since part of the business of these global companies runs in currencies other than the dollar), the expectations of profits of the sector and the interest rate environment. I will explain later some of these variables and their deep relationship with the ETF, but when justifying the thesis, today I position myself as hold.

The price of oil (Brent) is on a downward trend (previous image), although rising by 20% since the beginning of the year, partly due to the American operation in Venezuela and the uncertainty of the Iranian conflict. This increase has expanded the free cash flow of the production companies (upstream) by the increase in income received via price increases. This directly influences the ETF, since 72% of the holdings belong to the upstream category (17%) or have it integrated into their industrial process (53%). By this I mean that the price increase that the ETF has experienced has come from this factor.

As we will see later, there are contradictory signs such as the mentioned geopolitical problem with Iran, the increase in reserves by Venezuelan oil or the evolution of the DXY (a weaker dollar without episodes of risk off in the market, benefits the price of Brent). Therefore, and also counting on the evolution of the yield of the 10-year bond (increasing) and the sector breadth (using a U.S.-based proxy that is currently at highs), I am inclined at this moment for a hold of the position. Although there has been some rally in the price, I see some uncertainty in the medium term, where different “market forces” are fighting to influence the development of the price. From a breadth perspective, the market looks very bullish, a sign that it is not the best time to make a purchase.

ETF Overview

In the chart above, we can see the ETF’s key characteristics. It pays dividends semiannually and has 47 million shares outstanding, which together with its daily trading volume (over $400 million) makes it a highly liquid ETF. It was launched in 2001 and reports a 30-Day Median Bid/Ask Spread of 0.09%, a very low figure that adds only minimal trading friction.

The Portfolio

The ETF currently holds 50 securities in its portfolio and reports a 30 Day SEC Yield of 3.48%. This is a very correct figure for an ETF that seeks capital appreciation over time, without being an income-generating fund. Its beta equity in the last 3 years is 0.39, which means that in that period the ETF has moved less than the market. When the market as a whole moved 10% IXC did 3.9%. The P/E ratio of the ETF is 18.76, which is somewhat high when compared to the same average indicator of the last 10 years found in 14.8. On the cost side, the ETF has an expense ratio of 0.40%, which is acceptable for such an ETF.

Regarding the sectoral comment, we see in the images above as 55.45% of ETF exposure is within the Integrated Oil & Gas sector. This is followed by 16.90% in the exploration & production part (upstream) and 13.67% in storage & transportation. In the second image we can see the bias that the ETF has to the American energy industry. That is why we have included in the first section factors such as the price of brent or the evolution of the DXY. The fall in the latter constitutes a tailwind for both American and foreign industry, for reasons that I will explain below.

The table above shows the main holdings that the fund owns. We see that it has dominant positions in Exxon Mobil (19.65%), Chevron (10.65%) or Shell (6.86%). These first three companies have sounded like main actors to carry out the mission of restructuring and reorganizing oil production in Venezuela. With the corresponding investments and possible long-term margins that this may entail for the companies themselves.

Macro Drivers

The macroeconomic forces that drive the thesis of this article and the position (hold) of the analyst are the evolution of the price of Brent vs the DXY (Figure 1), the comparison of Brent vs the historical S&P 500 Energy (Figure 2) and the yield of the American 10-year bond (Figure 3). In the first graph we see how in the last 5 years both the price of the Brent futures and the DXY have moved hand in hand, the correlation in this period has been 0.58 explaining the movement of the other.

As we discussed earlier, the increase since the beginning of the year of Brent has directly influenced the expectations of the results of the different companies that the ETF owns. With the downward movement of DXY, the position of these companies has been strengthened in two ways: in the United States, making financial conditions more affordable and outside the United States, making demand for USD-denominated goods bought in other currencies cheaper.

Looking ahead, I expect a decline in the price of oil if geopolitical tensions with Iran are fixed, with a weaker dollar driven by the Trump administration. This scenario would be one of contrasting forces, on the one hand the decline in the dollar would benefit the ETF at the macro level for the reasons explained above and the decline in oil could compress somewhat the multiples of companies. This situation makes me award a hold to the ETF.

If we add the factor of the evolution of the yield of the American bond to 10 years, the picture becomes much clearer. As can be seen from the third chart, there has been a rise in bond yields since 2021, accelerating since the beginning of the year. This has been because of the fiscal uncertainty surrounding American debt and the way the Treasury funds itself on the short end of the curve.

This pushes the term premium on the long end higher, lifting long-dated yields. This increase is detrimental to the ETF and its companies because it adds to the discount rate applied to earnings (and cash flows). An increase in yield will be able to compress the company’s multiples and tighten long-term financial conditions, requiring the investor to reward more for taking on duration risk.

Market Breadth

Finally, we focus on a market breadth indicator that measures the state of the American energy market (61% of the ETF portfolio). The indicator marks today a signal in which 90% of the companies of the energy index have an active buying signal according to the technical indicator of point and figure (P&F).

We see how this very positive indicator coincides with a period in which the dollar has depreciated and both the price of Brent crude and the yield on 10-year bonds have increased. It is quite clear that the first two variables have contributed greatly to this trend since the beginning of the year.

Although this is indicative of the existence of a trend today, I believe that it will not last long due to the future evolution of the macro variables mentioned above (Brent price, DXY, and 10-year bond yield). This leads me to believe that we are facing a temporary sectoral rebound, which will disappear as capital rotation expands to other sectors.

Since I'm not a big fan of chasing price, I think the time at the technical level has passed, so I prefer to hold and wait for a market pullback to pull this indicator to a level close to 50 to buy at that level. Until then hold. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Buy Now, Pay Later: A Split Decision
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) services like Affirm (AFRM) and Klarna (KLAR) are becoming a go-to option at checkout, allowing shoppers to split purchases into smaller, often interest-free payments. Federal Reserve data shows younger users are more likely to use BNPL—and more likely to miss a payment. From an investment standpoint, analysts remain divided between Affirm and Klarna, while experts warn the convenience of BNPL can come with budgeting risks.

This is an abridged transcript.

As we embark on this holiday weekend, you’ve probably noticed the sales and maybe even already given into the power of advertisement and soon, likely next week, that special item will grace your door step in a box that’s being put together as we speak.

One of the options you may have noticed at check out was for Buy Now, Pay Later.

The concept isn’t new. Credit cards have been around since the mid-1900's. Essentially a way to buy now and pay later.

The New York Fed says Americans credit card debt has topped $1.28T.

But we aren’t talking about credit cards today.

We’re talking about that option you have when you buy something online and it allows you to choose how to pay. Credit or debit cards are always accepted. Some merchants also allow you to use an account that is built into your phone like Apple Pay (AAPL). Still others offer the option we’re focusing on today, Buy Now, Pay Later using Affirm (AFRM), Afterpay (XYZ), Klarna (KLAR) or Paypal (PYPL) to name a few.

Those companies allow the consumer to split their payments into smaller amounts, zero interest, no credit check. Usually, the set number of payments is four with no interest but if you need more time, you could be hit with interest, which the companies show you how much upfront.

So how do they make money? The retailers. The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis says the revenue for the Affirms and Klarnas of the world “comes primarily from the fees they charge retailers for using BNPL services.” It notes, “The fees are often higher than those charged by credit card companies, but retailers are willing to pay these fees to increase sales.”

So something must be working.

Buy Now, Pay Later is an option for regular everyday purchases like groceries, you can even pay for a vacation over time. Not to mention something that has been on repeat in my news feed for the past couple of weeks, rent now, pay later. Yes, you can now split your rent into two payments.

Millennials have been dubbed the Buy Now, Pay Later generation but the service is widely used by those 18 and older.

The Fed's latest information about the economic well-being of U.S. households was published last summer for 2024.

It showed that 19 percent of 18 to 29 year olds have used Buy Now, Pay Later. The same percentage holds true for those 30 to 44. The number drops to 16 percent for those 45 to 59 and 8 percent for 60 and older.

How many of them made late payments on a Buy Now, Pay Later program? For the youngest demographic, 32 percent. The next oldest, 25 percent. For those 45 to 59 the number drops to 21 percent who said they made a late payment. And for the oldest demographic, 12 percent made at least one payment after the due date.

There’s a question about whether the consumer’s financial house is in order based on their use of Buy Now, Pay Later.

Julia: So, first of all it definitely looks by the behavior of the consumer that the consumer might be weaker.

That’s Julia Ostian, an analyst at Seeking Alpha. She suggests that not everything is as straightforward as it appears.

Julia: Based on the numbers that were reported by Walmart or Amazon for example, and I was actually covering the recent earnings of both of these stocks, I didn’t see an actual sign for that.

Affirm’s CEO, Max Levchin, confirmed this on its Q2 earnings call earlier this month saying, the consumer they’re seeing to date is quite healthy. They're borrowing money and they’re willing to pay it back.

The New York Fed found that about 72% of financially stable users and 89% of financially fragile users had made multiple Buy Now, Pay Later purchases in a 12-month period. They caution that this puts the consumer at risk for overextending their budget.

The reasons for using these services include the fact that the payments are spread out, zero interest, the loans are easy to access, and it's convenient.

Julia: More people buying and selling on the internet, the more this system will be working, and the more this industry will be growing.

Which brings us to the investment aspect of things.

Julia says when you weigh Affirm (AFRM) against Klarna (KLAR), Affirm is a BUY in her opinion.

Julia: I have to say that Affirm is pretty special here and Affirm doesn’t have that high interest rate. Also, it has much less hidden fees than some of the newer and I could say maybe more progressive companies like for example Klarna that just had an IPO.

But not everyone feels that way. Out of all the most recent analysis there's one contrarian view. Merc Research on Seeking Alpha rates Affirm a Strong Sell. It says “with a 30% to 50% downside on AFRM, I maintain a SELL position due to Affirm's disadvantages in an increasingly multi-option checkout and larger-than-expected UK expansion challenges that will hinder the Street's lofty expectations.”

Affirm (AFRM) went public in January 2021. It was founded in 2012.

Affirm (AFRM) is priced at $54.26, down 27% YTD. It’s rated BUY by Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street. The Quant rating is HOLD.

Klarna (KLAR) was founded in 2005. It went public less than a year ago, September 2025.

Klarna (NYSE:KLAR) shares officially started trading on the New York Stock Exchange at $52 each, representing a 30% premium to the $40 initial public offering price.

As of the time of this recording, Klarna (KLAR) is trading at $18.34 per share. Klarna (KLAR) next reports earnings before the bell on February 19th.

Julia: It is not profitable yet and Affirm is for the last few quarters already.

She says Klarna (KLAR) right now is just risky business.

Julia: I really feel this way because of how risky Klarna still is. If the company at least would be closer to profitability or would show some of the more tangible results, let's say I would definitely rethink my decision and I think it will come really soon.

But Klarna (KLAR) could be a nice play.

Julia: If the stock goes down slightly more.

Juxtaposed Ideas on Seeking Alpha rates Klarna BUY with a caveat. The caveat is that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer, attributed to the potential capital losses from the ongoing correction.

That’s one analyst but overall, Seeking Alpha analysts rate Klarna a BUY, Wall Street also gives it a BUY rating. It is not covered by Seeking Alpha’s Quant system. YTD KLAR is down 36%.

Each company has relationships with retailers, some exclusive. They each offer a card that expands your payment options from just online purchases to also paying in-person. Klarna (KLAR) offers cash back for some merchants. Affirm’s cash back rewards program was discontinued a couple of years ago.

The bottom line:

Julia: Buy Now, Pay Later is a general lifeline for people who need to make a large purchase or even a purchase that currently is not sitting well on their bank account.

Catalyst watch:

  • The New York Toy Fair will take place over the weekend. Leading brands such as Hasbro (HAS), Mattel (MAT), Spin Master (TOY:CA), and Jakks Pacific (JAKK) will unveil their 2026 product lines and highlight new licensing partnerships tied to blockbuster entertainment releases.

  • It is the one-year anniversary of the SailPoint (SAIL) IPO.

As a reminder, Monday is a stock market holiday so no Wall Street Breakfast. I’ll see you again on Tuesday.

data.title: Preferred Stock ETFs Vs. Common Equity Income ETFs: Why PFFA Potentially Stands Out
data.description:
In an environment shaped by elevated interest rates and uneven equity performance, income-oriented investors often face a difficult choice. Dividend-paying stocks can provide growth potential but expose portfolios to market volatility, while bonds tend to offer stability at the expense of income. Preferred stocks occupy a less-discussed middle ground, combining elements of both asset classes in a structure designed to prioritize cash flow.¹

Preferred securities generally pay fixed dividends and rank senior to common equity in the corporate capital structure. Dividends must be paid to preferred shareholders before any distributions are made to common stockholders, and preferred holders also rank ahead of common equity in liquidation.² These features of preferred stocks often result in higher and more predictable income than common stocks, though with less upside participation.³ As a result, preferred stocks tend to appeal to investors focused on income stability rather than capital appreciation.

Following the U.S. Federal Reserve’s aggressive tightening cycle from March 2022 to July 2023, preferred shares were pushed to levels rarely seen over the past decade.¹ This shift renewed interest in preferred stock ETFs, particularly as many now offer yields that exceed those available from traditional equity income strategies. At the same time, preferred stocks carry distinct risks, including interest-rate sensitivity, call provisions that can cap returns, and heavy concentration in financial issuers.⁴ These characteristics make portfolio construction and security selection especially important in this segment of the market.

Preferred Stocks: Structure, Benefits, and Risks

Preferred shares are often described as hybrid securities because they blend equity ownership with bond-like cash flows.³ While they typically lack voting rights, their fixed dividend payments and seniority provide a measure of income stability that common stocks cannot. Historically, preferred dividends have exceeded those of common equity issued by the same company, reflecting this higher claim on corporate cash flows.⁵

That stability comes with trade-offs. Preferred prices generally move inversely with interest rates, and many issues are callable at the issuer’s discretion, which can limit upside when prices rise.⁶ Sector concentration presents another challenge, as banks and other financial institutions dominate preferred issuance.⁴ These structural risks mean that passive, index-based preferred ETFs may inherit vulnerabilities that are difficult to manage without flexibility.

Active Management and the Role of PFFA

The Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA, the Fund) takes a distinctly active approach to addressing these challenges. Rather than strictly tracking a preferred stock index, the fund actively selects securities, seeks to avoid unfavorable call structures, and dynamically allocates between fixed-rate and floating-rate preferreds based on interest-rate conditions.⁷ PFFA also diversifies beyond the bank-heavy composition common to most preferred benchmarks, reducing reliance on a single sector.⁷

Another defining feature of PFFA is its use of low-to-moderate leverage to enhance income generation. While leverage increases risk, it also allows the Fund to seek to deliver higher distributions than many passive peers.⁷ During periods of market stress, such as the regional banking turmoil of early 2023, PFFA’s active flexibility allowed it to reduce exposure to vulnerable issuers while passive funds remained fully invested.⁴

Preferred dividends often qualify for favorable tax treatment, enhancing after-tax income for many investors.⁸ Over time, we believe PFFA’s combination of active security selection, sector diversification, and tactical risk management has translated into competitive long-term results relative to broad preferred benchmarks.⁷

Balancing Yield and Prudence

PFFA is not without risk. Leverage can amplify drawdowns, and prolonged high interest rates could pressure preferred valuations. Still, for investors willing to accept a more sophisticated strategy, PFFA offers a differentiated approach to income investing. By actively managing the unique risks embedded in preferred securities, the Fund provides an alternative to traditional dividend equity ETFs that emphasizes income resilience over market beta. In a yield-constrained environment, that distinction can be meaningful. For investors seeking high income and capital appreciation, we believe PFFA can be a useful tool.

Footnotes

  1. Dave Gilreath and JR Humphreys, “An Advantageous Time for Preferred Stocks,” Medical Economics, June 6, 2025.

  2. Adam Hayes, “Preferred vs. Common Stock: What’s the Difference?” Investopedia, January 17, 2025.

  3. Adam Hayes, “Preferred Stock,” Investopedia, January 10, 2025.

  4. InfraCap Funds, “The Potential Benefits of Active Management with the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA),” October 12, 2025.

  5. Adam Hayes, “Dividend,” Investopedia, January 8, 2025.

  6. Adam Hayes, “Callable Preferred Stock,” Investopedia, January 11, 2025.

  7. Infrastructure Capital Advisors, “$PFFA: The Active Preferred ETF Edge,” October 18, 2025.

  8. Internal Revenue Service, “Qualified Dividends,” December 6, 2024.

data.title: Baron Discovery Fund Q4 2025 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Dear Baron Discovery Fund Shareholder,

The fear of machines turning evil is [a] red herring. The real worry isn’t malevolence, but competence. A super-intelligent AI is by definition very good at attaining its goals, whatever they may be, so we need to ensure that its goals are aligned with ours... If we cede our position as smartest on our planet, it’s possible that we might also cede control.- Life 3.0: Being Human in the Age of Artificial Intelligence , Max Tegmark, pp. 67-68.

Today, many of us choose to live in a form of pseudo-reality governed by algorithmically enabled individual experiences. Much of what passes for authentic experience today is vicarious and virtual... [W]hat began as a slow bleed of reality on the edges has now become a culture-wide destabilizing force. Reality has competition, from both augmented and alternative forms... In these new worlds, we are Users, not individuals.- The Extinction of Experience: Being Human in a Disembodied World , Christine Rosen, pp. 2-4.

You do not rise to the levels of your goals,you fall to the level of your systems.- Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones , James Clear, p. 27

Throughout my career I’ve had my antennae up, looking for examples of people who use systems as opposed to goals. In most cases, as far as I can tell, the people who use systems do better... To put it bluntly, goals are for losers. That’s literally true most of the time.- How to Fail at Almost Everything and Still Win Big , Scott Adams, pp. 31-32

The above quotes seem to be strange juxtapositions, but actually mesh together pretty well, and are very apropos to the world in which we live right now. As a society, we are at the cusp of massive advances in the realm of AI. We hope that this will be to the benefit of humanity, but right now is the time in which humanity will determine the parameters (goals) around which AI algorithms are ultimately limited in their negative abilities. At the same time, we are at a crossroads in the manner in which we interact not only with the world, but with the machines that are increasingly blurring what is real and what is artificially created. This is an existential rubicon demarcating the very essence of which we define ourselves as human beings. Are we merely “Users” who are social vassals to our algorithmically driven devices (and the social media virtual realities that they convey)? Or do we have the power to deterministically (via systems) lead ourselves to brighter humanistic and societal outcomes?

Laird and I are huge optimists, and we believe that the companies in which we invest (and more importantly the people that run them) are being systemically built for positive outcomes that will make every generation more prosperous, healthy, and intelligent than ever before. Goals may indeed be for losers (let that be the AI machines). Being systems-based might be the key characteristic that distinguishes humanity from the relentless goal seeking of AI models.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For performance information current to the most recent month end, visit BaronCapitalGroup.com or call 1-800-99-BARON.

Critically, the process we use to manage Baron Discovery Fund (the Fund) is also systemic and is keyed to strictly adhering to our core long-term growth investing principles. Our target of earning 15% compounded returns over time is merely the fruit of our “system.” We invest for the long term in competitively advantaged companies that are well managed and have the potential of large untapped market opportunities. We then continuously monitor these companies and their competitors via our due diligence process. We allocate our overall portfolio via our risk process which includes balancing index sectors, types of growth, position sizing, and valuation parameters. We also spend many hours with the teams that manage the companies in which we invest, to gauge their mettle, morality and ability. No algorithm can substitute for this “live” research.

Performance

In the fourth quarter of 2025, the Fund returned 0.19% (Institutional Shares), trailing the Russell 2000 Growth Index (the Index) by 1.03%. For the full year 2025, the Fund returned 10.96%, and trailed the Index by 2.05%.

On every rolling five-year period since we started the Fund, our returns (Institutional Class) have beaten the market 93% of the time. Over the last 12 years, our process has led to very consistent annualized returns for the Fund of 12.61%, which has outpaced the Index by 3.81% annually. And this is AFTER management and other fees. So definitionally, our investors have meaningfully outperformed small-cap index funds.

Of course, this strategy will not work in the short periods of time in which the market is fixated on “hype” versus objective long-term valuation metrics. This is what occurred in spectacular fashion in the third quarter of 2025, and to some extent in the fourth quarter as well. In these periods, low quality (high debt and poor profitability) and short-term price momentum-oriented stocks outperformed. Because low quality stocks outperformed, our portfolio experienced a 4.7% headwind versus the Index in 2025 just for owning higher quality stocks! This was the worst year on record for underperformance by high quality* stocks (going back to when data first started to be collected in 1975). We doubt it will re-occur. Similarly, short-term momentum* was a 2.8% headwind to the Fund in 2025 versus the Index due to our long-term orientation. Stacked together, these “factors” hurt the Fund’s relative performance versus the Index by about 7%, putting the Fund’s overall 2% relative performance shortfall into some context. We plead guilty to not owning quantum computing companies with minimal revenues until the 2030s (or small nuclear reactor companies with the same profile). Not owning some of these speculative areas hurt us (relatively). This has just been the nature of the stock market for the second half of 2025. History informs that this should almost certainly change.

Portfolio Structure

Top 10 holdings
